turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: I have the opinion of the Court in case 15-8049, Buck versus Davis. Duane Buck shot and killed his former girlfriend, Debra Gardner, and her friend, Kenneth Butler. The Texas jury convicted Buck of capital murder. Under state law, the jury could impose the death penalty only if it found that Buck was likely to commit acts of violence in the future. Buck's lawyer called a psychologist who had examined Buck, Dr. Walter Quijano, to offer his opinion on that issue. Dr. Quijano had prepared a report concluding that on balance Buck was unlikely to be a future danger. But Dr. Quijano's report also stated that Buck, who was black, was statistically more likely to act violently because of his race. Buck's counsel put the report into evidence. Dr. Quijano reiterated his views when he testified, identifying race as a factor known to predict future violence. The prosecutor followed up on cross-examination asking Dr. Quijano whether “The race factor, black, increases future dangerousness.” Dr. Quijano replied, “Yes.” The jury sentenced Buck to death. Buck's was not the only case in which Dr. Quijano testified that a defendant's race weighed in favor of a finding of future dangerousness. In 1999, one of the other cases reached this Court on a Petition for Certiorari. The State of Texas agreed with the defendant in that case that he was entitled to a new sentencing proceeding free from Dr. Quijano's race-based testimony. Soon afterward, the Texas Attorney General issued a public statement identifying six similar cases, including Buck's, in which Dr. Quijano had testified. In five of those cases, the state confessed error and consented to resentencing, but not in Buck's. Buck now contends that his lawyer's introduction of Dr. Quijano's testimony violated his right to the effective assistance of counsel under the Sixth Amendment. This claim has never been heard on the merits in any court. When Buck first challenged his conviction and sentence in a state habeas proceeding, his lawyer failed to raise any claim based on Dr. Quijano's testimony. And when Buck tried to assert this claim in a subsequent federal habeas petition, the district court held that Buck had defaulted the claim by failing to raise it in his first state habeas proceeding. That decision was perfectly proper under the law as it stood at that time. But later decisions from this Court modified the default rules. Had those cases been decided before Buck filed his federal Petition for Habeas Corpus, he could have proceeded with his Sixth Amendment claim. Now, if Buck was going to benefit from this change in the law, he would have to find a way to reopen his federal case. So he filed a motion under a provision in the federal rules that authorizes a court to reopen a closed case in “extraordinary circumstances.” In arguing that his case was extraordinary, Buck relied on, among other factors, the change in the law governing default, the introduction of expert testimony linking Buck's race to violence, and the fact that Texas had confessed error in the other cases in which Dr. Quijano had testified. The district court denied relief. The court concluded that Buck had failed to show extraordinary circumstances because the introduction of race into his sentencing had only been de minimis. Buck wanted to appeal that decision to the Court of Appeals. Now, to do so, he was required to get what is known as a certificate of appealability from the Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit. A certificate of appealability is an order saying that the applicant has made a substantial showing of the denial of a constitutional right. Not that he would win, just that he had shown enough to get in the door. The Court of Appeals denied Buck's application. Buck sought review of that decision in this Court, and we now reverse. To begin, the Fifth Circuit's basic approach to deciding whether Buck deserved a certificate of appealability was flawed. When a prisoner seeks a certificate of appealability the Court of Appeals may engage in only a limited inquiry. The court's job is to ask whether reasonable jurists could disagree with the district court's resolution of the prisoner's claims. If so, a certificate must issue, and then the court goes on to hear the appeal in the normal course. Here, the Fifth Circuit exceeded the limited scope of this analysis by essentially requiring Buck to show that he was entitled to relief on the merits before he could get in the door. Buck should have been granted a certificate of appealability and allowed to proceed with his appeal. We now turn to the merits of that appeal. Buck's claim of ineffective assistance of counsel under the Sixth Amendment required him to make two showings. The first is that his counsel performed deficiently. We have no trouble concluding that Buck has shown deficient performance. His trial lawyer knew that Dr. Quijano's report stated that Buck's race predisposed him to violent conduct. And he knew that the only real point of the dispute during the penalty phase of the trial was Buck's future dangerousness. Buck's life or death hung on the resolution of that issue. Yet his counsel called Dr. Quijano to the stand and put his report into evidence. That was incompetence. The second showing Buck had to make was of prejudice. He had to show a reasonable probability that he would not have been sentenced to death but for his counsel's errors. He has made that showing. The jury in Buck's case was required to make a prediction about whether Buck would be violent in the future. This determination required a degree of speculation. The jury heard that Buck had committed violent acts in the past, but these had occurred outside of prison and in the context of romantic relationships with women. If the jury did not impose a death sentence, Buck would be sentenced to life in prison where no such relationship would arise. In light of these changes, a juror might conclude that Buck would not act violently in the future and spare his life. But Buck's race would never change. And in Dr. Quijano's view, this immutable trait made it more likely that Buck would act violently in the future. To the jury, this was hard statistical evidence offered by an expert witness to guide an otherwise speculative inquiry, and it was potent evidence. Dr. Quijano's testimony appealed to the racial stereotype that black men are prone to violence. This confluence of factors convinces us that the district court was wrong to conclude that the effect of Dr. Quijano's testimony on Buck's sentencing was de minimis. It is true that there were only two references to race in Dr. Quijano's testimony. One during direct examination and the other on cross, but when a jury hears expert's testimony that expressly makes a defendant's race directly pertinent on the question of life or death, the impact of that evidence cannot be measured simply by how much airtime it received at trial or how many pages it occupies in the record. Buck has shown prejudice, and with it, ineffective assistance of counsel. We also conclude that Buck has shown the extraordinary circumstances required to reopen this case. There is a reasonable probability that Buck was sentenced to death in part because of his race. This represents a striking departure from a fundamental principle of our criminal justice system. Our law punishes people for what they do, not who they are. Dispensing punishment on the basis of an immutable characteristic flatly contravenes this guiding principle. The remarkable nature of Buck's claim is confirmed by the state's own response to Dr. Quijano's testimony in other cases. It is not every day that a state seeks to vacate the death sentences of five defendants convicted of capital murder. And although Texas has attempted to explain its decision to treat Buck differently from the other five defendants identified in the Attorney General's public statement, its justifications for distinguishing Buck's case are simply unrelated to the Attorney General's stated reason for confessing error that, “It is inappropriate to allow race to be considered as a factor in our criminal justice system.” The high standard for reopening a closed judgment reflects the importance of finality in litigation. But the state's decision to confess error in other cases in which Dr. Quijano testified reflects its own judgment that the people of Texas have no interest in enforcing such a tainted capital sentence. In concluding that respect for finality does not require us to preserve the district court's judgment, we do no more than acknowledge what Texas itself recognized 17 years ago. Accordingly, the judgment of the United States Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit is reversed and the case is remanded for further proceedings consistent with the opinion of the Court.
